abstract:
Pushing data traffic from cellular networks to WiFi is a primary example of inter-radio access technology (RAT) offloading. While this strategy clearly alleviates congestion on overloaded cellular bands, the ultimate potential of such offloading and its systemic impact on overall performance are not yet fully understood. To address this, we develop a general and tractable framework consisting of $M$ different RATs. Each RAT can deploy up to $K$ distinct tiers of access points (APs) that vary in transmit power, path loss exponents, deployment density, and available bandwidth.

In our model, each class of APs is represented as an independent Poisson point process (PPP). Mobile user locations are modeled as a separate, independent PPP, and all wireless channels incorporate i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. We then derive the distribution of data rates across the entire network using a weighted association strategy. These weights can be specifically tuned to optimize different network objectives. Our analysis reveals that the optimal fraction of traffic to be offloaded to maximize Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) coverage does not, in general, align with the fraction required to maximize rate coverage—defined as the percentage of users achieving a specific minimum data rate.
